submission_text: Aristotle's virtue ethics, as articulated in the Nicomachean Ethics, proposes that flourishing (eudaimonia) is the result of cultivating virtuous character over time. Unlike Kant's deontology, which locates moral worth in adherence to universal principle (Kant, 1785/1993, p. 41), Aristotle takes a developmental view: virtues are habits formed through practice (Aristotle, trans. Ross 1925, Book II). In contrast to utilitarian aggregation (Mill, 1863), Aristotle's approach is agent-centered. Recent commentary by Hursthouse (1999) argues that virtue ethics offers more nuanced moral guidance than rule-based systems. The essay continues with original argument...
